section.title: The Trial, Crucifixion, and Burial
section.sectionRationale: Handed to Pilate and shuttled to Herod, Jesus is repeatedly declared innocent, yet the crowd demands Barabbas and cries for crucifixion until Pilate yields. On the way He tells the weeping women to weep for themselves, and at the Skull He is crucified between two criminals, praying, Father, forgive them. A dying thief believes and is promised Paradise. Darkness falls, the temple veil is torn, and Jesus commits His Spirit to the Father and dies. A centurion glorifies God, and Joseph of Arimathea lays Him in a new tomb as the Sabbath begins.
section.thesis: The innocent Son goes to the cross in the place of the guilty, forgiving His killers, opening Paradise to a dying thief, tearing the veil that barred the way to God, and entrusting His Spirit to the Father, so that His death is the innocent dying for the guilty and the way to God flung open.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the trials before Pilate and Herod; the choice of Barabbas and the sentence; the way to the cross and the daughters of Jerusalem; the crucifixion and the penitent criminal; the death of Jesus with the torn veil; and the burial by Joseph of Arimathea.

units.label: Before Pilate and Herod
units.note: I find no basis for a charge against this man

verses.text: Then the whole council rose and led Jesus away to Pilate.
And they began to accuse Him, saying, “We found this man subverting our nation, forbidding payment of taxes to Caesar, and proclaiming Himself to be Christ, a King.”
So Pilate asked Him, “Are You the King of the Jews?” “You have said so,” Jesus replied.
Then Pilate said to the chief priests and the crowds, “I find no basis for a charge against this man.”
But they kept insisting, “He stirs up the people all over Judea with His teaching. He began in Galilee and has come all the way here.”
When Pilate heard this, he asked if the man was a Galilean.
And learning that Jesus was under Herod’s jurisdiction, he sent Him to Herod, who himself was in Jerusalem at that time.
When Herod saw Jesus, he was greatly pleased. He had wanted to see Him for a long time, because he had heard about Him and was hoping to see Him perform a miracle.
Herod questioned Jesus at great length, but He gave no answer.
Meanwhile, the chief priests and scribes stood there, vehemently accusing Him.
And even Herod and his soldiers ridiculed and mocked Him. Dressing Him in a fine robe, they sent Him back to Pilate.
That day Herod and Pilate became friends; before this time they had been enemies.

units.label: Barabbas and the sentence
units.note: he handed Jesus over to their will

verses.text: Then Pilate called together the chief priests, the rulers, and the people,
and said to them, “You brought me this man as one who was inciting the people to rebellion. I have examined Him here in your presence and found Him not guilty of your charges against Him.
Neither has Herod, for he sent Him back to us. As you can see, He has done nothing deserving of death.
Therefore I will punish Him and release Him.”
But they all cried out in unison: “Away with this man! Release Barabbas to us!”
(Barabbas had been imprisoned for an insurrection in the city, and for murder.)
Wanting to release Jesus, Pilate addressed them again,
but they kept shouting, “Crucify Him! Crucify Him!”
A third time he said to them, “What evil has this man done? I have found in Him no offense worthy of death. So after I punish Him, I will release Him.”
But they were insistent, demanding with loud voices for Jesus to be crucified. And their clamor prevailed.
So Pilate sentenced that their demand be met.
As they had requested, he released the one imprisoned for insurrection and murder, and he handed Jesus over to their will.

units.label: The way to the cross
units.note: do not weep for Me, but for yourselves

verses.text: As the soldiers led Him away, they seized Simon of Cyrene on his way in from the country, and they put the cross on him to carry behind Jesus.
A great number of people followed Him, including women who kept mourning and wailing for Him.
But Jesus turned to them and said, “Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for Me, but weep for yourselves and for your children.
Look, the days are coming when people will say, ‘Blessed are the barren women, the wombs that never bore, and breasts that never nursed!’
At that time ‘they will say to the mountains, “Fall on us!” and to the hills, “Cover us!”’
For if men do these things while the tree is green, what will happen when it is dry?”

units.label: The crucifixion and the penitent criminal
units.note: today you will be with Me in Paradise

verses.text: Two others, who were criminals, were also led away to be executed with Jesus.
When they came to the place called The Skull, they {{stauroo|crucified}} Him there, along with the criminals, one on His right and the other on His left.
Then Jesus said, “Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.” And they divided up His garments by casting lots.
The people stood watching, and the rulers sneered at Him, saying, “He saved others; let Him save Himself if He is the Christ of God, the Chosen One.”
The soldiers also mocked Him and came up to offer Him sour wine.
“If You are the King of the Jews,” they said, “save Yourself!”
Above Him was posted an inscription: THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS.
One of the criminals who hung there heaped abuse on Him. “Are You not the Christ?” he said. “Save Yourself and us!”
But the other one rebuked him, saying, “Do you not even fear God, since you are under the same judgment?
We are punished justly, for we are receiving what our actions deserve. But this man has done nothing wrong.”
Then he said, “Jesus, remember me when You come into Your kingdom!”
And Jesus said to him, “Truly I tell you, today you will be with Me in {{paradeisos|Paradise}}.”

units.label: The death of Jesus
units.note: Father, into Your hands I commit My Spirit

verses.text: It was now about the sixth hour, and darkness came over all the land until the ninth hour.
The sun was darkened, and the {{katapetasma|veil}} of the temple was torn down the middle.
Then Jesus called out in a loud voice, “Father, into Your hands I {{paratithemi|commit}} My Spirit.” And when He had said this, He breathed His last.
When the centurion saw what had happened, he gave glory to God, saying, “Surely this was a righteous man.”
And when all the people who had gathered for this spectacle saw what had happened, they returned home beating their breasts.
But all those who knew Jesus, including the women who had followed Him from Galilee, stood at a distance watching these things.

units.label: The burial of Jesus
units.note: a new tomb, where no one had yet been laid

verses.text: Now there was a Council member named Joseph, a good and righteous man,
who had not consented to their decision or action. He was from the Judean town of Arimathea and was waiting for the kingdom of God.
He went to Pilate to ask for the body of Jesus.
Then he took it down, wrapped it in a linen cloth, and placed it in a tomb cut into the rock, where no one had yet been laid.
It was Preparation Day, and the Sabbath was beginning.
The women who had come with Jesus from Galilee followed, and they saw the tomb and how His body was placed.
Then they returned to prepare spices and perfumes. And they rested on the Sabbath, according to the commandment.

stauroo.gloss: To crucify, to put to death on a cross. The most shameful and agonizing of Roman executions, reserved for slaves and rebels, becomes the means of the world's salvation. The King of the Jews is crucified between two criminals, numbered with transgressors, bearing in His body the curse that fell on sinners.

paradeisos.gloss: Paradise, a garden of blessing, the presence of God. Jesus promises the dying, believing thief that this very day he will be with Him in Paradise. Salvation comes by simple faith at the last hour, apart from any works, and the assurance is immediate: to be with Christ is Paradise itself.

katapetasma.gloss: The veil, the great curtain that shut off the Most Holy Place. At the moment of Jesus' death it is torn down the middle, signaling that the barrier between God and sinners is removed. Through the torn body of Christ a new and living way into God's presence is opened for all who believe.

paratithemi.gloss: To entrust, to commit into another's keeping. Quoting Psalm 31, Jesus dies not in despair but in trust, committing His Spirit into the Father's hands. Even in death He is the faithful Son, entrusting Himself to the God who will raise Him, a pattern of trust for all who die in Him.

basic.context: The council brings Jesus to Pilate, charging Him with subversion and claiming to be a king. Pilate finds no guilt and sends Him to Herod, who mocks Him and sends Him back. Three times Pilate declares Him innocent, yet the crowd demands the release of Barabbas, a murderer, and shouts for Jesus to be crucified until Pilate gives in. On the way, Jesus tells the wailing women to weep for themselves and their city. At the Skull He is crucified between two criminals and prays, Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing. The rulers, soldiers, and one criminal mock Him, but the other criminal believes and is promised, today you will be with Me in Paradise. Darkness falls, the temple veil is torn, and Jesus commits His Spirit to the Father and dies. A centurion praises God, and Joseph of Arimathea lays Him in a new tomb as the Sabbath begins.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the whole trial and crucifixion through Luke's insistence on Jesus' innocence: Pilate, Herod, the penitent thief, and the centurion all testify He has done nothing wrong. The innocent One dies in the place of the guilty, symbolized by Barabbas released and the thief forgiven, and the torn veil declares the way to God now open through His death.
basic.originalLanguages: The King is stauroo, crucified, the shameful death of rebels. To the believing thief He promises paradeisos, Paradise, that very day. At His death the katapetasma, the temple veil, is torn open. And He dies in trust, quoting Psalm 31, as He paratithemi, entrusts, His Spirit to the Father.
basic.typology: Barabbas the guilty released while the innocent dies pictures substitution, the sinner freed because Christ takes his place. The torn veil fulfills the tabernacle's barrier, now opened. The darkness and the righteous sufferer echo Psalm 22 and the prophets, and Jesus is the true Passover Lamb slain as the feast begins.
basic.christ: Jesus is the innocent King crucified for the guilty, who forgives His killers and opens Paradise to a dying thief. He is the true sacrifice whose death tears the veil, and the faithful Son who dies entrusting His Spirit to the Father, the righteous man the centurion confesses.
basic.god: God's saving purpose stands even through corrupt courts and a manipulated crowd. God accepts the innocent in place of the guilty, opens the way to His presence through the torn veil, and receives the Spirit of His trusting Son. His justice and mercy meet at the cross.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 foretells the innocent servant who bears the sin of many. Psalm 22 is the psalm of the mocked, pierced sufferer whose garments are divided. Psalm 31 supplies the dying words, into Your hands I commit My spirit. Hebrews 10 explains the torn veil as the new way into God's presence.
basic.oneStory: At the cross the innocent Son dies for the guilty, the barrier to God is torn away, and a dying criminal enters Paradise by faith alone. The story reaches its darkest and brightest point together: the Lamb is slain, the veil is opened, and the trusting Son is laid in a tomb, waiting for the dawn of resurrection.

context.literary: Luke's passion narrative stresses Jesus' innocence through repeated verdicts and the words of the penitent thief and the centurion. Distinctive to Luke are the trial before Herod, the address to the daughters of Jerusalem, the prayer of forgiveness, the promise of Paradise, and the committing of the Spirit. The account moves in stages from trial to sentence to cross to death to burial, ending as the Sabbath begins.
context.historical: Pilate, the Roman governor, alone could impose the death penalty, which is why the council needed him. Crucifixion was Rome's most degrading execution. Barabbas was a real insurrectionist and murderer. The temple veil separated the Most Holy Place. Burial before sundown on Preparation Day, and the women's plan to return after the Sabbath with spices, reflect Jewish law and custom.
context.canonical: The cross fulfills Isaiah's suffering servant and the righteous sufferer of the Psalms, and it inaugurates the new access to God that Hebrews expounds through the torn veil. The substitution pictured in Barabbas and the thief is the heart of the gospel Paul preaches. The burial in a new tomb sets the stage for the resurrection that vindicates it all.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify that Jesus died innocent, condemned by injustice yet according to God's saving plan, and to reveal His death as the substitution of the righteous for the guilty that forgives enemies, opens Paradise to the believing, tears the veil to God, and is embraced in trusting obedience to the Father.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The events are narrated as the once-for-all saving death of Christ, not as a pattern to repeat. Yet the penitent thief models saving faith, Jesus' prayer of forgiveness and trusting death shape His people's dying and forgiving, and the torn veil prescribes bold access to God through Christ.

debates.question: What does the promise of Paradise to the thief teach about salvation?

views.label: Salvation by grace through faith alone, apart from works
views.case: The thief can do nothing but believe and appeal to Jesus; he has no time for baptism, penance, or good works, yet Jesus assures him of Paradise that very day. The scene displays justification by faith at the eleventh hour, resting wholly on Christ and His grace.

views.label: The immediate blessedness of being with Christ after death
views.case: The word 'today' also teaches that the believer is with Christ at once upon death; there is no long sleep or delay before entering His presence. To die in faith is to be with the Lord in Paradise immediately, a comfort for all who trust Him.

debates.bearing: Both truths stand together: the dying thief is saved by faith alone in the crucified King, and to be with Christ, that very day, is Paradise, so no repentant sinner who turns to Jesus is ever too late.

debates.question: What does the tearing of the temple veil signify?

views.label: The opening of the way into God's presence
views.case: The veil barred access to the Most Holy Place; its tearing at Jesus' death declares that through His sacrifice the barrier between God and sinners is removed, and all who come by faith may now draw near. Hebrews reads it exactly this way, a new and living way opened through His flesh.

views.label: A sign of judgment on the temple and its order
views.case: The torn veil can also signal that the temple's day is ending, its holiness exposed and its function fulfilled, as Jesus becomes the true meeting place with God and the temple faces the destruction He foretold. The sign points both to open access and to the passing of the old order.

debates.bearing: Whether stressing access or judgment, the torn veil proclaims that through the death of Christ the old barrier is gone and God is now approached through the crucified Son.

study.originalLanguages: The King of the Jews is stauroo, crucified, subjected to the most shameful death Rome could devise, reserved for slaves and rebels, and so numbered with transgressors even in the manner of His dying. To the criminal beside Him who turns and believes, Jesus promises paradeisos, Paradise, the garden of God's presence, and promises it for that very day, so that salvation is shown to come by bare faith at the last hour, apart from any work. At the moment He dies, the katapetasma, the great temple veil that shut sinners out of the Most Holy Place, is torn down the middle, declaring that the barrier to God is removed through His death. And Jesus dies not in despair but in trust, taking Psalm 31 on His lips as He paratithemi, entrusts, His Spirit into the Father's hands, the faithful Son commending Himself to the God who will raise Him.

typology.type: Barabbas the guilty released while the innocent is condemned
typology.fulfillment: Substitution: the sinner set free because Christ dies in his place, Luke 23:18-25.

typology.type: The veil barring the Most Holy Place, Exodus 26:33
typology.fulfillment: The veil torn at Jesus' death, opening the way to God, Luke 23:45.

typology.type: The righteous sufferer mocked, pierced, and stripped, Psalm 22
typology.fulfillment: Jesus mocked, crucified, and His garments divided by lot, Luke 23:34-37.

study.christ: Jesus is the innocent King, declared guiltless by Pilate, Herod, a dying criminal, and a Roman centurion, who nonetheless goes to the cross in the place of the guilty. From the cross He prays for His killers, Father, forgive them, and opens Paradise to a thief who can offer nothing but faith. He is the true Passover Lamb slain as the feast begins, the sacrifice whose death tears the veil and throws open the way to God. He dies as the faithful Son, entrusting His Spirit to the Father in the language of the Psalms, and He is buried in a new tomb, the righteous One laid in death, awaiting the vindication of resurrection. Every detail proclaims the innocent dying for the guilty.
study.god: The God of the cross works His saving purpose through the failures of human justice, the cowardice of Pilate, the mockery of Herod, and the fury of the crowd, without being the author of their sin. He is the God who accepts the innocent in the place of the guilty, releasing the Barabbas in every sinner because His Son takes their condemnation. At the death of Jesus He Himself tears the veil, opening His presence to all who come by faith, and He receives the entrusted Spirit of His obedient Son. Here His justice against sin and His mercy toward sinners meet and embrace, and the darkness over the land is the shadow of a love that spares nothing to save.

crossReferences.note: The servant pierced for our transgressions, the innocent bearing the guilt of many, fulfilled at the cross.
The mocking of the sufferer and the dividing of His garments, echoed in Luke 23:34-35.
Into Your hand I commit my spirit, the trusting words Jesus speaks as He dies.
We have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, through the curtain, that is, His flesh.

study.oneStory: At Golgotha the innocent Son dies for the guilty: Barabbas walks free, a thief enters Paradise by faith alone, and the veil that shut sinners out of God's presence is torn from top to bottom. The darkest hour of the story is also its brightest, for here the Lamb is slain, sin is borne, and the way to God is opened. The trusting Son commits His Spirit to the Father and is laid in a new tomb as the Sabbath falls, the whole creation holding its breath for the third day.

quiz.q: What verdict do Pilate and Herod reach about Jesus?

options.text: That He is innocent, having done nothing deserving of death.
options.note: Luke 23:4,14-15,22 repeatedly declares Jesus not guilty.

options.text: That the charges against Him were unproven.
options.note: True, but weaker than Luke's repeated, emphatic declaration of His innocence.

options.text: That He was guilty but did not deserve death.
options.note: They find no guilt at all, not merely a lesser guilt.

options.text: That He was clearly guilty of inciting rebellion.
options.note: The opposite: they find no basis for the charge of rebellion.

quiz.q: What happens when the crowd is given a choice?

options.text: They demand the release of Barabbas, a murderer, and the crucifixion of the innocent Jesus.
options.note: Luke 23:18-25 shows the guilty freed and the innocent condemned.

options.text: They call for Jesus to be crucified.
options.note: True, but it omits the pointed contrast of Barabbas the murderer set free in His place.

options.text: They ask Pilate to decide for himself.
options.note: They actively demand Barabbas and shout for crucifixion, not a neutral deferral.

options.text: They ask for both men to be released.
options.note: They demand Barabbas released and Jesus crucified.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to the penitent criminal?

options.text: He promises, today you will be with Me in Paradise, saving him by faith alone at the last hour.
options.note: Luke 23:43 assures the believing thief of immediate Paradise.

options.text: He assures the man that he will be remembered in the kingdom.
options.note: True to the man's request, but Jesus promises far more: Paradise that very day.

options.text: He tells the man to prove his repentance by his deeds.
options.note: The thief can do no works; he is saved by faith alone, not by proving himself.

options.text: He remains silent to the criminal's plea.
options.note: Jesus answers with a direct promise of Paradise.

quiz.q: What happens at the moment of Jesus' death?

options.text: Darkness covers the land, the temple veil is torn, and Jesus commits His Spirit to the Father.
options.note: Luke 23:44-46 records the darkness, the torn veil, and His trusting last words.

options.text: Jesus cries out and breathes His last.
options.note: True, but it omits the darkness and the torn veil that interpret His death.

options.text: Jesus dies quietly with no accompanying signs.
options.note: There are dramatic signs: darkness and the tearing of the veil.

options.text: The temple is immediately destroyed.
options.note: The veil is torn, not the temple destroyed; that judgment comes later.

quiz.q: What does the tearing of the temple veil signify?

options.text: The barrier between God and sinners is removed, opening the way into His presence through Christ's death.
options.note: The torn veil declares open access to God, as Hebrews 10 explains.

options.text: The old temple order is passing away.
options.note: True as a secondary sense, but the primary meaning is the opened way into God's presence.

options.text: The temple curtain was simply worn out.
options.note: The tearing is a divine sign tied to Jesus' death, not natural wear.

options.text: God had abandoned His people entirely.
options.note: The opposite: the way to God is now thrown open through Christ.

questions: Barabbas the guilty walked free because the innocent Jesus took his place. How does it change you to see yourself in Barabbas, released because Christ was condemned?
From the cross Jesus prayed, Father, forgive them. Is there someone you consider beyond forgiveness, and how does His prayer confront your own unforgiveness?
The dying thief was saved with no time for works, only faith. Does that comfort you or unsettle you, and what does it say about how anyone is saved?
The veil was torn so you could come near to God. Do you live as one shut out, or as one welcomed freely into His presence through Christ?
Jesus died entrusting His Spirit to the Father. What would it mean for you to face your fears, and finally death itself, with that same trust?

translationNotes.note: The BSB omits verse 17, moving from verse 16 to 18, because the earliest manuscripts do not contain it. Some later manuscripts add, 'Now Pilate was obligated to release to the people one prisoner at the feast,' drawn from Matthew 27:15 and Mark 15:6. The custom it describes is still evident in the crowd's demand for Barabbas.

Some manuscripts do not include 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.' The BSB retains this well-loved saying, which fits Luke's portrait of Jesus praying for His enemies even from the cross.
Jesus' dying words quote Psalm 31:5, 'Into Your hand I commit my spirit.' He dies not in despair but in trust, entrusting Himself to the Father who will raise Him.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 23:1-56. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, poem markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Verse 17 is absent from the BSB, following the earliest manuscripts, and is carried in the translationNotes. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
